title: Chapter 11: Gravitation - Study Notes
source: A Quick Review of E-PCM for Engineering Entrance - Physics

sections.heading: Newton's Law of Gravitation
sections.content: Gravitational force is attractive, central, and conservative. It acts along the line joining two masses.
formulas: g inside earth (uniform density): g proportional to r
specialCases: If earth mass is constant, g is proportional to 1/R^2.
For identical spheres in contact: F proportional to 1/r^2 with r = radius.

sections.heading: Variation of g (Height, Depth, Rotation)
sections.content: Acceleration due to gravity changes with altitude, depth, and earth rotation.
formulas: For h << R: g_h approx g(1 - 2h/R)
Apparent g due to rotation: g' = g - omega^2 R cos^2(phi)
specialCases: At equator, g is reduced by omega^2 R.
At pole, rotational reduction is zero.

sections.heading: Gravitational Field and Potential
sections.content: Field is force per unit mass; potential is work per unit mass.
formulas: Inside hollow sphere: E = 0, V = constant = -GM/R
At center of uniform sphere: V = -3GM/(2R)
specialCases: Potential is continuous across spherical shells.
Field at center of shell is zero.

sections.heading: Satellites and Orbits
sections.content: Orbital motion is governed by gravitation providing centripetal force.
specialCases: Geostationary orbit: T = 24 hr, r approx 4.24e7 m, h approx 3.6e7 m.
If speed increases beyond circular value, orbit becomes elliptical; at escape, parabolic.

sections.heading: Escape Velocity and Binding Energy
sections.content: Escape velocity is the minimum speed required to reach infinity with zero final speed.
formulas: If v = n v0, speed at infinity: v_inf = v0*sqrt(n^2 - 1)
specialCases: At escape velocity, total energy is zero.
Escape speed from moon is lower than from earth.

sections.heading: Kepler's Laws
sections.content: Kepler's laws describe planetary motion in elliptical orbits.
formulas: Law of orbits: planets move in ellipses with sun at a focus
Law of areas: areal velocity is constant
Law of periods: T^2 proportional to a^3 (a = semi-major axis)
specialCases: For circular orbit, a equals orbital radius.
For ellipse with r1, r2: a = (r1 + r2)/2.
